entry.title: 36::7 - Photo code
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Speaking of photo processing workflow, here's the only photo I took on Jan 16th: a snip of some of the code that pre-processes my photos when I plug in my cameras or phone or whatever various other photo devices to my computer. This helps me merge photos from all of my various cameras and devices into Lightroom in a way where I can verify uniqueness in the case of disaster recovery or when duplicates somehow creep in. Regarding duplicates, I use ZFS with deduplication on the backup side of things so even if duplicates interfere with my photo workflow, they don't take up much more room on my disks.
WarzauWynn posted a photo:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

uZnpPD

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Starting another 365 project and digging into photos that are nearly two months old has renewed my happiness that I took the time to learn to code. Organizing photos would take an unsurmountable amount of time if I hadn't automated so much of my workflow. I highly recommend that everybody who does not know how to make computers do things for them take the time to learn how to do so. Even knowing just a little bit about programming goes a long way. Check out Code Academy if you are looking for a free, easy intro into making your computer work for you so you can get back to working on things you love, like taking photos.

entry.title: 35::182 - Vincent's 2nd day in USA
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Tien's good friend and college classmate Vincent arrived in America yesterday and the head of a two year student visa and Fulbright Scholarship studying in West Virginia. He and the other Fulbright students are taking a month to study at the Monterey Institute of International Studies in Pacific Grove before heading on to their collegiate destinations. Tien and I took our third anniversary trip down to Monterey to coincide with his arrival, welcome him and see some sights together. Here they are at the Bixby Bridge.

This man and his little entourage frequently stand at Powell Station by the cable car turntable and shout loudly at people about how we're all going to burn in hell. He and his crew try to coerce people to join their religious sect by vocal intimidate, taunting and condemning. I've never seen anybody say anything nice to him, and am curious if he's ever brought somebody to Jesus with his tactics.

entry.title: 35::137 - Tien in dim light
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I'm amazed at the VR performance of the 70-200mm VR ii. You can shoot it hand-held at 1/(focal length/10), blowing away the traditional hand-held rule. This shot is 200mm @ 1/20s hand-held, and I can't see any motion blur when zoomed 1:1.

entry.title: 35::132 - Pinhole of a prism
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

A long time ago I took a Nikon body cap an drilled a big hole in it with a dremel tool. Then I cut open an aluminum can and taped part of it over the hole. Then i poked a hole in it with a sewing needle. This created a very tiny aperture. This is how you make a pinhole camera. The aperture was too small though, not enough light was coming through, and you could tell it wasn't circular. Finding something only slightly larger than a sewing needle was difficult, so today I decided to just push a thumb tack through it. This created a very nice round hole, but it was too large to make a very clear image. In any case, this is a photo I took using a piece of an aluminum can as a lens on my D800. Maybe next I'll dry tin foil, that's supposed to work well.

entry.title: 35::123 - Nate at Rickhouse
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Tonight I got together with my friend Nathan for the first time in forever. We passed through Rickhouse on our way around downtown and had a sip of Ardbeg Corryvreckan. I hadn't been to Rickhouse since I had my D3s, so it was interesting to see how well the low light performance of the D800 compared. I think it did spectacularly, and although I really love the D3s, I gotta say I think the D800 is better overall.

entry.title: 35::119 - Zapcar
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Sometimes you zip, sometimes you zap.
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I'd been throwing around the idea of picking up a f/1.2 lens, but the thought of having another MF lens wasn't all that awesome. My friend Dan picked up the Canon 50mm f/1.2L AF and after looking at how great the images were I thought I'd finally pick one up, so I found this pre-AI Nikkor 55mm f/1.2 S•C on CL. It just barely fits on my D800, but I'll have to have it converted in order for it to function well. It's too bad that Canon is the only manufacturer who makes f/1.2 AF lenses.

entry.title: 35::59 - Tien at the Stockton crossroads
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I picked up this Nikkor 28mm ƒ/2.8D lens off Craigslist today for $150 and went straight out to take pleasing photos with it. I was really happy to find an AF wide prime for a decent price. I'm a little bummed that Nikon hasn't made any cheap AF-S wide primes, their only offerings cost many hundreds of dollars and are large, heavy things. That's one reason I've been shooting the 35mm DX on my D800 so often, but now I have this even wider FX so that's rad.

entry.title: 35::55 - Infinity Bliss
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

The D800 has an in-cam multiple exposure feature. You can do anywhere from two to ten exposures with the same settings. This photo was taken with three exposures at 1 second each. For the first two I focused on the Infinity Towers, then took the camera and moved it sinuously upwards. The third exposure is a stationary, defocused shot of the Infinities.

D5200 is a really great camera. I highly recommend it for people who want an SLR without going too high on price.

entry.title: 35::11 - Raymond, Tien and Olive
entry.summary: WarzauWynn posted a photo:

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

There's a speed boat behind that wave, but you can't see it because the wave is so large.

I got my D5200 today and this was one of the 19th photo I took with it. I'm already enjoying it as much as any other camera with the flip-out LCD form factor, but it has a feature I've long wanted and never seen on any camera I've owned: an option to automatically set the minimum shutter speed based on focal length when shooting with auto-ISO. This feature was available in the T3i, but at the expense of manual minimum shutter speed selection. The D5200 has both, and that is a beautiful thing. (I later found that the D800 also has this feature but I'd missed it.) This image was taken with that feature enabled. The camera wouldn't let the shutter time go below 1/500s with such a long zoom on a cropped sensor, so it upped the ISO to 250 to keep the shutter time fast. I didn't have to choose 1/500s, the camera selected that based on my focal length.

On the down side, Nikon really needs to implement focus peaking like the Sony NEX has. Live-view benefits so much from focus peaking, especially with the flip out screen where you're doing weird angles and need visual feedback of precise focus, eg: ground-level macros. They should be doing everything they can to get whatever legal rights they need to get in order to have that feature.
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

entry.title: 35::2 - Facetime with Ben and Thu
entry.summary: WarzauWynn posted a photo:

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I'm 35 today. This is the beginning of my fourth 365 project.

If you're considering starting a 365 project, do it. It is very personally satisfying and much easier than writing in a journal. I treat mine as a documentary so there is no pressure to be creative on days when I am in a funk. I wish I had started years earlier.

entry.title: 34::364 - January by the bridge
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

On Dec 26 I dropped my D800 onto the carpet from merely 2 feet. I thought nothing of it, but realized soon afterwards that my 24-70mm was damaged enough that I can't use it anymore. Tonight I noticed that the body was not focusing correctly when not used in live view. The fact that $5000 worth of photo gear was damaged by a mere two foot drop onto carpet is extremely disappointing for me. I won't be taking many photos with this camera until it's fixed, and I don't know when that will be.

entry.title: 34::309 - Tarkhuna

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Last night I got into a conversation about liquor and wine with my landlord when he spotted me coming in with a brown bagged bottle. He showed me his wine cellar where he had a variety of wines and several spirits. He gave me this mostly empty bottle of Tarkhuna. It is probably the best vodka I've ever had, so smooth. entry.title: 34::263 - Rollerblading Violinist
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I was down at Embarcadero to photograph Critical Mass 20, but I was early and decided to leave when I found this girl rollerblading around by the California Street cable car turntable playing Moon River. I took a video and talked to her for a minute. I thought she might be like Gabriel Angelo, a music student who gets hours of practice in while making a little money and getting his name out, but that wasn't so. She had been in America visiting her father and was now traveling back to Vienna. As I took this photo she was playing Ein Klein Nachtmusik, quite appropriate for a girl from Vienna.

entry.title: 34::234 - Rocket with Pureview
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

My coworker Ali loaned me a Nokia Pureview. Its camera is very remarkable, but the rest of the phone is pretty lackluster. Plus, who wants a Meego phone anyway? Certainly not for more than $500, especially since that price demands RAW image support. Hopefully this sensor comes to other phones.

entry.title: 34::233 - Mithun Pal, August 2012
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Mithun and I went next door to The Gap HQ and tried to get up to their terrace, which we'd heard was public access, but were refused by the guards. To my surprise Mithun whipped out his mobile phone and called up his elite contacts on the inside to get us past security and up to the grand view of the Bay Bridge above the bow and arrow. Quite a delight.

entry.title: 34::224 - My Steed
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I've done around 2k miles on this thing, I think it's time for a tune up. Muni has been so awful lately and the bright side of that is I've gotten the motivation to start biking to work again. Incidentally, it takes less time for me to ride my bike across the entire width of San Francisco than it would for me to take the L or the 48 to the subway. Less commute time coupled with a workout and a cost of zero seems like a great deal to me.

entry.title: 34::216 - Tien at Fort Funston
entry.summary: WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I've been really inspired by Kim-Ngan Ao's photo stream, particularly the double exposures. I've been doing more of those since I got my D800, and today Tien and I sat for a while analyzing silhouette photos and talking about how to go about composing double exposure shots in regards to shapes, patterns and light.

entry.title: 34::214 - Saint Thomas the Apostle Roman Catholic Church
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Oh Christian God, please smite Muni with your mighty vengeance so that they may learn the error of their deceitful ways. Teach them that when a bus is scheduled to show up, it is a sin for one to not show up, but it is an abomination to miss two consecutive ones.

On a happier note, if either of the two 18's that were scheduled to show up had actually done so, I wouldn't have taken this photo. Also, if the river of the 31 hadn't completely ignored me when I motioned for her to open the door back up I wouldn't have gotten as good of a photo, because I took this right after she drove away and left Tien and I standing there in the cold windy fog.

entry.title: 34::175 - Pacific Bell retrofit
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

From what I understand, this is a seismic retrofit of the historic PacBell building. It's a beautiful building, I've always wanted to take photos inside of it. I'm glad they're repairing it so it can survive the next big quake. According to Wikipedia, this is the future home of Yelp.

Picked up this 18-55mm DX lens yesterday and was amazed at how well it worked on the D800 in FX mode. At 18mm it looks heavily vignetted like this, but from 22mm on it's much more gently stylized.

This shot had LR4's lens profile distortion correction applied, but not vignette removal.

entry.title: 34::101 - First IR Portrait
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Today marks a new milestone for me. I shot Nikon for 15 years, then switched to Canon to see what it was all about. After shooting for nearly a year, I've decided that Canon is over-hyped, and I simply don't like it as much as Nikon. With that, I'm switching back to Nikon, and doing so in style. This morning I picked up a Nikon D70, the first model of DSLR I ever owned, which had been converted to IR by Life Pixel. Like so many other people I'm waiting for my D800 order to be fulfilled, so this D70 should keep me occupied until then. I also picked up a 45mm PC-E ƒ/2.8 tilt-shift lens, which is something I've been missing since I got rid of my PC-E 24mm ƒ/3.5. As of today I'm back on Nikon, doing what I love, not just exploring the alternatives. So here's a photo of my lovely wife with light eyes and blue hair.

Tonight, Tien and I went into LX to meet up with some of the AGU crew for what they call the Bullshit Speaking Club where we all eat dinner, drink beer and enjoy the company of other english speakers. We got there before anybody else though, so we went shopping at the night market.

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

When Tien and I came out of Vietnamese class we immediately spotted this big black butterfly fluttering back and forth between two trees.

entry.title: 34::35 - Finish
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I was studying Vietnamese and Nhi was dancing with the Kinect. They've danced to every song we have a million times, it would be nice if some other interesting Kinect games would come out that have more content. We only have a few and they're getting played out.
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

entry.title: 34::33 - Little cousinettes
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Tien and I went with some of her family to visit her cousins in Long Xuyen. I always find myself paying attention to the children because vocal communication isn't such a large part of interacting with them, so we can still have fun despite speaking different languages.

entry.title: 34::32 - At a wedding party
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Tien and I went to a wedding party for a friend that she went to Jr. High school with and hadn't seen in over 10 years. It's interesting to me that in VN culture that is what happens, you invite the whole village to your party. The party was at a nice hotel next to the Coop Mart in Long Xuyen. The groom spoke english, which was nice because I could congratulate him myself.

entry.title: 34::31 - Toàn Thắng
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

This massage parlor only employs blind people, allowing them to have a normal job where they participate in society and earn their money rather than begging on the streets. Tien and I both got full body massages after studying today.

entry.title: 34::30 - Sisters, young and old
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

We went through old photos today and I found this one of Thu and Tien when they were little girls that was really adorable. I had them pose with it so we can see them young and old.
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

entry.title: 34::27 - The fresh produce department
entry.summary: WarzauWynn posted a photo:

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

More and more I am disappointed by the colors I get from the T3i. I'm not 100% sure that it's the T3i though, it could be that I'm editing photos on the less-than-amazing screen on the 11" MBA. I don't think so though, because it's much easier to get the colors right on images taken with other cameras. In any case, I'll be posting a lot of B&W until I get rid of this T3i.

entry.title: 34::25 - Tien's self portrait cake
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Today was Tien's 29th birthday, and since there's no cake shop in Binh Hoa, and since it's difficult for me to do things in secret, she ended up buying her own cake. She picked this one out because she said it looked like her, which I thought was absolutely adorable.

Last year was dinner at Bobo's Steakhouse, this year was dinner on the kitchen floor.

entry.title: 33::349 Minh and Thu say goodbye
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

They've been married for 5 years, but America still won't let her into the country. He came back for her interview at the consulate hoping she'd come back to America with him, but with no specific reason (which is typical) they said they couldn't give her a visa yet, so once again she's left behind as he heads back to the USA, on Christmas Eve no less.
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

entry.title: 33::345 - Over Rach Gia
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

The boat ride out to Phu Quoc was so turbulent that many people on board were throwing up repeatedly for more than an hour. Tien didn't fare quite that bad, but also was seasick, so we decided to fly back to the mainland.
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

entry.title: 33::343 - Seasons greetings from Bai Sao
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

We rode down to Bai Sao today and found it almost unrecognizable as the placid paradise we discovered a year and a half ago. The wind was strong, the waves were large, the ocean was upset, and the beach had been reshaped into a short, steep drop directly down to the water. Apparently we had hit it at the perfect season last time. Still, it was gorgeous and we had a good time.

It was my joke this year that our white Christmas would involve white sandy beaches, but it was true!

entry.title: 33::342 - Red lights on the river
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Tien and I arrived at Phu Quoc today. We checked into our old hotel, took a nap and went shopping. We ended up at the night market for dinner. This was taken where the night market street ends at the river.

entry.title: 33::341 - Sunset in Rach Gia
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

We missed the boat to Phu Quoc by 5 minutes, so we spent the night in Rach Gia.

This could be a scene from nearly any Vietnamese pop song. They're all sad and heartbroken.

entry.title: 33::325 - Tien at SFO

entry.title: 33::298 - In that bloodshot eye
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I've been playing with the Rokinon 85mm on two sets of Fotodiox tubes. The Rokinon is nice for its manual aperture, but it's not very clear at high apertures, which is less than suitable for macro. Still, it's not a bad setup. I wasn't totally happy with this shot, but for a 365 it'll do.

entry.title: 33::289 - Obama's entourage
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Obama's motorcade rolled into my neighborhood today shortly after the police towed every vehicle whose driver was stupid enough to miss seeing the no parking signs, including my scooter. We weren't allowed to go out the front door or open the windows of our building. I had to go out the back and walk to the impound lot, since the bus route had been cut off as well, and pay $450 to get my scooter back. Maybe I should start riding a bicycle.

Video on my youtube page.
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

entry.title: 33::232 - The Expected
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

One of the Anonymous slogans is "Expect us." The cops and the BART riders expected Anonymous tonight, so much so that most commuters went home early leaving mostly protestors and cops parading up and down Market street. I walked with them for a while to take photos, video, and stand up for freedom of speech.

There's something awesome about a man in a pin striped suit riding a bicycle, even without a mask and a megaphone.

Videos of the protest are on my youtube

entry.title: 33::218 - muBARTek
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Towards the end of work I heard sirens and helicopters, and knew that Anonymous had indeed come to town. Ah, the good old days of masked men on market street swinging around light poles, skipping along and telling everybody to not be afraid because they are from the internet. Except this time it wasn't like that. It was interested crowds, shouting protestors, stoic riot cops, and a lot of irritated commuters.

Video footage here: www.youtube.com/watch?v=zU8T-zxNGSA

entry.title: 33::216 - New camera

This is my new camera toy. I've finally gotten a grasp on how best to use it to take the videos I want, but also found some neat still photo tricks to do with it as well. If you want to see my videos, don't look here! Video doesn't belong on Flickr, imho. Head on over to my Youtube page for my videos.

This photo was taken with the 85mm Rokinon on a short extension tube for macro focus, a setup that's working out really well for me.

entry.title: 33::176 - Installing Lion, drinking beer
entry.summary: WarzauWynn posted a photo:

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

On my way to work I spotted this crazy handlebar configuration, passed it, then walked back to photograph it.

Lately I've been using the T3i's liveview in manual focus mode with the Sigma 30mm ƒ/1.4 and have been very happy with the results. You have to move the focus square to where you want it in the composed photo, then tap the zoom-in view button twice to get a full-scale focus view. This lets you nail your focus and your composition simultaneously. This technique works on other cameras as well, and in fact was exactly how I used the D300 and D3s with the PC-E 24mm in tilt/shift mode to get proper TS focus.

entry.title: 33::154 - Image Number 0
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

The first shot of the Canon T3i was actually numbered 0. Self portrait because, hey, it's got a flip-out screen so why not?

So far, I think that for me, switching off the D3s to a newer, cheaper camera was a good change. I do not think that the D3s is $4500 better than this camera. One thing I'm enjoying already is the 50% more megapixels this camera offers. One thing I'm already missing is the killer tone curve and details in the dark areas the D3s had. Aside from that, experiencing photography from a consumer standpoint on a new platform is refreshing. The feature set is different, the interface is different.

One other thing I love about the T3i so far is that if I leave the LCD closed, it's just like using a film camera. All the important info is in the viewfinder.

Now, I need to accessorize with some great glass, because that's the most important part of a camera system these days.

entry.title: 33::116 - New toys
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

No, the iPhone isn't new, but the S95 that took this photo is, and so is the Eye-Fi card. Before going out and buying an Eye-FI "Pro" card, keep in mind that even the Eye-Fi "Pro" card cannot geotag RAW files, so you end up having to shoot RAW+JPG then run exiftool to migrate the exif data from the JPG to the RAW. The import tool is written in AIR though, so I have no idea why they don't give you the option to store RAW as DNG and include the GPS data there.

Also, so far my experience with this device reminds me of why I love Apple and buy their products. Speaking of Apple, the ability to transfer files to my iPhone so far does not work. Eye-Fi has been incredibly disappointing so far because it won't Geotag the file format I want, and it won't transfer to my phone, which are the two reasons I bought this thing.

It's really feeling like spring in San Francisco, and the weather today was the best it's been in months. Tien and I went out to Cafe Greco for breakfast and then strolled through the art show in Washington Square where we found the Saints Peter and Paul Church undergoing some kind of construction.

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

First photo posted from a Nikon 105mm DC lens that I picked up off craiglist in a cash and lens swap for my 24mm PC-E.

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Today was a really busy day, I snapped this outside my house as I was getting home from work.

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I brought out one of my old favorite lenses that I never use anymore, the Sigma 10-20mm. It's a cropped sensor lens, so it's funky on the D3s, but even before I got a full frame camera I had backed off of the wide-angle perspective quite a bit. I've taken a lot of photos with this lens, many that I'm proud of, but I just lost interest in that perspective. I'm thinking about selling it, but not before I take it out to give it one last go.

entry.title: 32::365 - Brief recline
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

And thus we end my first 365 project with my wife reclining in throw-away furniture found on the streets of Chinatown in San Francisco. I plan to keep taking daily photos, and there's still no goal other than seeing where I was each day. Maybe this year I can remember to photograph the friends and acquaintances I encounter while we're talking instead of remembering to do so after we've already parted ways. I also hope to have this year span at least five countries, whereas I think this year was only four, but I don't yet have plans to visit any that I've not already been to.

I have just a few days left of being 32, and a few days left in my 365 project. I'm going to continue it for the next year and I'm thinking of ideas on what to do. I'm thinking of doing themed weeks.

entry.title: 32::353 - Pump
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

My aunt found this old pump that belonged to my grandfather, who was an engineer. He had a lot of cool things like this in the garage, but it burned down almost completely many years ago so most of it was lost. This pump still works fine as a sort of bellows, and the first thing I thought was "that'd be an awesome steampunk costume prop."

I was using this prop to teach Tien how to shoot lightbox product shots, moving from a prime to a zoom and finally to a tilt lens where you can align the focal plane to suit your needs. As soon as I put my PC-E 24mm on my D3s I realized that with that crop factor that lens is really not good for small product shots. I wonder if I should trade it in for a 45mm or 85mm...

This was one on-cam bounced SB-900 as master to a remote SB-900 in a 32" Westcott softbox from above.

entry.title: 32::AM::309 - Cable Car Crime Scene
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I hear cops all the time, that's only natural living 3 blocks from the police station, so I was a little alarmed to walk outside and see this scene shortly after hearing some sirens on my block. All the SFMTA guy would tell me is that it was some crime involving a passenger and a gesture that looked to me like a passing vehicle. So, basically I don't know what happened. The cops said something about clothes and the handle of the cable car that controls its grip on the cable, and it looked like a woman and a man were involved. Rice-a-roni was unavailable for comment as their hours are 7:30am - 5:00pm central time Monday through Friday.

I came home and found my rear stairwell destroyed.

entry.title: 32::AM::291 - Giant orange pills

The pills are working good too, got my hearing back within an hour of taking them, but lost it again today, so I'm keeping on them.

entry.title: 32::AM::283
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

This is near the top of Russian Hill, a few blocks from my house. Tien and I walk up here sometimes to get a little workout, but I never bring my camera along at those times. Tonight we decided to walk up just to take this photo. I did it in HDR with Photomatix 4, but it looked lower quality than this and I didn't like it as much. More and more I think HDR is unnecessary in most circumstances, and other than the bright lights of Broadway here, that is certainly the case with this shot.

This is not HDR. This photo is a single exposure developed entirely in LR3.

entry.title: 32::AM::268 - $75 shot
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

The plane was on its descent, banking into a good view of my lens. I was framing up the shot when Tien tapped me on the shoulder and motioned... I looked to my left and there was a park ranger motioning for me to move. I nodded, looked back through my camera and took this shot. With that, the ranger got out of his vehicle and issued me a ticket, saying he would not have done so if I wouldn't have taken that last photograph. I thought that was kind of silly since there were about 10,000 people around for the air show violating all sorts of traffic laws, and there I was, color blind and parked in a red zone without knowing it.

entry.title: 32::AM::262 - Natural light
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I say that if you want to shoot in natural light, you have to shoot in a place with advantageously natural lighting. If that lighting is under control, set it up advantageously and leave it, then when it comes time to shoot it's natural.

entry.title: 32::AM::261
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I recently got a message about my 3d photos, so I decided to whip out the old toy lens with some flash action for my 365 shot today. With ƒ/11 it took two SB900's at M 1/2 to fill the room with light, triggered via CLS.

I still wish the Loreo had a wider ocular separation and a wider field of view. This shot here is probably the best perspective for the Loreo. Any closer and it won't focus, much farther away and you lose the 3d effect.

entry.title: 32::AM::260

entry.title: 32::AM::258

Her tastes have adapted quite a bit to the foods that are available in America, and things like pizza and sushi which she used to deplore are now among her favorites.

entry.title: 32::AM::257

entry.title: 32::AM::256
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Tonight was the date of the mid-autum festival which coincides with the rising of a large harvest moon just before the sun sets. Tien and I missed the harvest moon rise, but managed to catch the sunset from Coit Tower. We hope to spend this day in Vietnam in a year or two. Tien says that couples light candles and float them off down the river in paper boats.

entry.title: 32::AM::234 - Slide razor
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Ever since I got my first pocket knife when I was 5 years old I've always had a trusty knife around me. This is a design that is completely different from any other knife I've had, but it's turning out to work pretty well. It uses disposable razor blades, so this is essentially a glorified box cutter. The great thing about it is there's no sharpening involved, and if you break the tip, who cares?

entry.title: 32::AM::232
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Tien and I woke up to our brand new, less than 24 hours old scooter laying on the sidewalk with its front left blinker and rear brake handle broken off. Somebody must have tried to wheel it away it and then dropped it when they saw the lock on the back wheel. That was lame, but it didn't stop us from scooting around SF for most of the day before meeting up with my old friend Vince later that evening. We drank beers and talked about music long into the night.

entry.title: 32::AM::231 - Zero miles
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Tien and I were walking to the bus today when we passed Golden Gate Cycles. There were several motorbikes parked outside, luring us in. So, we went in and looked around and rode off on a Yamaha Vino 125. I'd had my eye on them for a while, and $1000 off was a great deal, so what the heck? We scooted around town for a bit before picking up some Golden Boy Pizza, taking it to Savoy Tivoli and having pizza and a few drinks while listening to live jazz. Tien reacted badly to something she consumed, either the pizza or the half of a martini that she drank, and felt ill so we went home. It was a great day. :)

entry.title: 32::AM::215 - San Francisco Municipal Gym Membership Card
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Sometimes in San Francisco, busses are late. Or they just never come. Sometimes they're early. Any way you look at it, riding Muni involves walking and running. Just the other day I was heading home, but after seeing Google's prediction for the next bus being 15 minutes out, I decided to start walking along the bus line instead of just standing there. I would look back, making sure to not be too far from a bus stop without time to find one. 45 minutes later I arrived at my house, not a single bus had passed by on that route. I looked back and one was just coming up from the bottom of the hill. This is why waiting for busses in San Francisco is a bad idea. It's best to just start walking and get on that bus if it happens to come by. Great way to get in shape, and it's only $60 a month.

Seeing this photo after posting it makes me wish I'd brought along an 85 prime... maybe I should buy one...?

The white sand made for awesome lighting, and there was absolutely no haze. A polarizing filter is what made the sky so blue.

entry.title: 32::AM::78
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

Like many people on earth, coffee is a regular part of my life. I have this obsessive compulsive thing that I do with these standard American to-go coffee cups. I have to take the sissy mitt and align its diagonal seam over the vertical seam of the cup, opposite the drinking hole of the lid. I think I came to do this by experiencing too many dribbles through a gap if the seam of the cup aligned near the drinking hole.

Photography-wise, I was hoping to align the focus p lane of the seam with the pulley in the background, but the plane wouldn't tilt that far at such a close focus with decent composition. Sometimes when things go wrong, I quickly finish taking the photo, flaws and all, and admit that photography is not science, it's art. It's an art with a science behind it, but it's still art, and art cannot be perfect.

entry.title: 32::AM::65 - The wall in my office
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I started on this 365 project with the idea that over this year I would begin making a living from photography. 66 days in I am photographing the wall of the office where I work in IT. It highlights my doubts about my photography as I wonder where I stand in this field, why I do it, and whether or not I should continue. This evening I thought long and hard about giving up photography altogether and finding something else with more value, something I could live off of.

Thinking about it that way I am dejected by where I am compared to where I wanted to be, but still inspired. The next few months are merely a setup for the months that will follow, they're not the feature of this year.

entry.title: 32::AM::21 - 26th Avenue
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I know a lot of people love the 18-200mm ƒ/3.5-5.6 VR, but for me it's always too fuzzy and I tend to not use it anymore. I took it out this morning and reminded myself why with several shots that were even more fuzzy than this one. I think the problem is the zoom mechanism dragging the focus mechanism when I zoom in and out to recompose after zooming all the way in to fine-focus.

I set my exposure to manual, and since the PC-E lenses are manual focus it was a full manual day like I used to shoot every day a decade or so ago... with the bonus of live-view.

entry.title: 32::AM::13
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

I nearly cheated on my 365 shot for today, and although I didn't I'll explain why I nearly did. This is the window in the lobby of Groundwork Open Source where I've been doing some work lately. It's gorgeous, every time I walk by I have to stop and see the new ways that the sky has changed. I took this shot in the morning to fulfill my 365, but in the evening the light was spectacular for about an hour and I took a few shots that I'm much much more happy with, and incidentally required much less post production development. I was going to post one of the evening photos, but decided to stay true to my morning theme. I'll post the other shot too in a day or so.

The waves on the ocean were amazing, but I couldn't really capture them with the lens I'd brought. When I got to Mocha 101 it was inexplicably closed, which was very lame. I found another cafe up the street and got a little work done. Still in search of technologist work in SF or SE Asia, and of course photographic work.

entry.title: 32::AM::8 - No Tree X

WarzauWynn posted a photo:

No, there is no tree, only a sidewalk where once a tree grew wild.

I applied for a technologist position in Singapore today, which would be very interesting, exciting and new. The photography stuff isn't picking up in SF like I'd hoped, so I'm back to exploring all my options. Moving to Asia would surely make my AM::32 project more interesting.

entry.title: 32::AM::5 - Coding
entry.summary:
WarzauWynn posted a photo:

As much as I love to get outside, some mornings I just roll out of bed, crawl over to my computer and resume whatever I was doing the night before, in this case writing perl code to find duplicate files from a recovered hard disk crash. I've sorted through about 10,000 triplicate files so far, clearing up tons of hard disk space and more importantly, organizing my LR2 library. I do miss having an actual office though... or even a desk, for that matter.

entry.title: 32::AM::4 - The kitchen table
entry.summary: WarzauWynn posted a photo:
